sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 59, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The gray block at (28,28) is adjacent to black block at (31,28) - this blocks RIGHT movement. The gray block at (31,25) is also adjacent to black at (31,28) - might block DOWN too. Need to test if DOWN from (28,25) to (34,25) works.
- Plan: Send UP, RIGHT, RIGHT to reach (28,25), then try DOWN to (34,25) to see if that path works.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 21 actions; a strong score needs about 16 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
DOWN worked! The player moved from (28, 25) to (34, 25). So the DOWN path works, even though the gray block at (31, 25) is adjacent to a black block.
This means my earlier hypothesis was wrong. The player CAN move through gray blocks that are adjacent to black blocks, as long as the movement is vertical (DOWN), not horizontal (RIGHT).
Or maybe the rule is different. Let me think about this.
From (28, 25), RIGHT to (28, 31) failed (game over). But DOWN to (34, 25) succeeded.
The difference is:
- RIGHT: passes through (28, 28) g, which is adjacent to (31, 28) B
- DOWN: passes through (31, 25) g, which is adjacent to (31, 28) B
Both gray blocks are adjacent to the same black block at (31, 28). But RIGHT failed and DOWN succeeded.
Maybe the rule is about the direction of movement relative to the black block. When moving RIGHT, the player passes through (28, 28) which is directly above the black block at (31, 28). When moving DOWN, the player passes through (31, 25) which is to the left of the black block at (31, 28).
Or maybe the rule is simpler: the player can move DOWN through gray blocks adjacent to black blocks, but not RIGHT.
Let me continue exploring from (34, 25). The player is now at (34, 25). From here, the player can move RIGHT to (34, 31), then RIGHT to (34, 37).
Let me check the blocks at row 34:
- (34, 25): W
- (34, 28): g
- (34, 31): W
- (34, 34): g
- (34, 37): W
So from (34, 25), the player can move RIGHT to (34, 31), passing through (34, 28) g.
Let me check if (34, 28) is adjacent to any black blocks.
Looking at the adjacency list:
- 26: (34, 28) g - adjacent to 19, 25, 27
Id 19 is (31, 28) B. So (34, 28) is adjacent to the black block at (31, 28).
But the player moved DOWN through (31, 25) g, which is also adjacent to (31, 28) B. So maybe the player can move through gray blocks adjacent to black blocks, as long as the movement is not RIGHT.
Let me test by sending RIGHT from (34, 25) to (34, 31).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: DOWN from (28,25) to (34,25) succeeded! Player now at (34,25). The bottom row has blocks at (34,25), (34,28), (34,31), (34,34), (34,37). Need to test if RIGHT works on the bottom row.
Plan: Send RIGHT actions from (34,25) to explore the bottom path toward the goal.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at (34, 25)
# Try RIGHT to (34, 31), then RIGHT to (34, 37)
# Then try UP to reach the goal area

# RIGHT 1: (34, 25) -> (34, 31)
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"RIGHT 1: ({cr}, {cc}), go={last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
        break

# RIGHT 2: (34, 31) -> (34, 37)
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"RIGHT 2: ({cr}, {cc}), go={last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
        break

# Try UP from (34, 37) to (28, 37) - but (28, 37) is the red block!
# Let's try UP and see what happens
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        cr = sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"UP: ({cr}, {cc}), go={last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
RIGHT 1: (34, 31), go=False
RIGHT 2: (34, 37), go=False
UP: (28, 37), go=False
